segments.s107-g89: ازان پس (literally 'thereupon then') is a poetic temporal phrase meaning 'after that'; یکی انجمن ساختند is a past-tense, third-person-plural clause: ساختند (they made/formed) governs انجمن (an assembly) with یکی ('one' / 'a single') in front as its quantifier. Word order is typical Persian: temporal phrase first, then the quantified object (یکی انجمن) and the verb at the end.
segments.s107-g90: ز گفتار او دل بپرداختند places a prepositional phrase (ز گفتار او, 'from/of his speech') before the verbal phrase دل بپرداختند. دل is the direct object/affected noun 'heart(s)' and بپرداختند is past-plural 'they paid/turned/devoted' (here 'gave their hearts to' / 'attended to'). The word order (prepositional phrase → object → verb) is emphatic/poetic but grammatically standard in epic style.
segments.s107-g91: نشستند و گفتند با یکدگر is a simple coordination: two past verbs (نشستند 'they sat down', گفتند 'they spoke/said') joined by و, followed by an adjunct with با یکدگر 'with one another' indicating mutual conversation. Note: نشستند is the past plural of 'to sit', and با یکدگر marks reciprocity.
segments.s107-g92: که introduces an indirect question/explanation clause here ('asking/that'), followed by از بخت ما (literally 'from our fortune' = 'by our fate') and چه آمد به سر ('what came to [our] head' = 'what happened to us'). آمد is past tense 'came' used idiomatically as 'befell/occurred'. را after ما is the object/topic marker giving emphasis to 'us'.
segments.s107-g93: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). شهریار is the subject 'the king'; این سخنها 'these words' is the definite/near demonstrative object; که گفت is a relative/relative clause marker plus past verb 'he said' (i.e., 'that he said'). Together: 'If the king (shall) ... these words that he said ...'.
segments.s107-g94: به می‌خوردن اندر نخواهد نهفت is an archaic/poetic phrasing: به + می‌خوردن functions as a verbal-nominal phrase related to drinking/ingesting (here 'in drinking' or 'while drinking'), اندر 'in/within', and نخواهد نهفت is a negative future/operative clause ('will not hide'—نخواهد as future/modal 'will not', نهفت 'hidden/conceal'). Grammatically the verb phrase (نخواهد نهفت) closes the conditional introduced by اگر.
segments.s107-g95: ز ما و ز ایران برآمد هلاگ is a compound nominative/verb clause: ز + noun ('from us and from Iran') marks source, برآمد is past 'arose/occurred', and هلاگ is the affected nominal 'destruction/perdition'. The order (sources first, then verb, then noun) is typical in the epic register for emphasis.
segments.s107-g96: نماند برین بوم و بر آب و خاک is negation plus locative listing: نماند is past negative sense 'did not remain / there remained none'; برین بوم ('بر' + 'این' written as برین) means 'upon this land'; و بر آب و خاک lists additional domains 'upon the water and the soil'. The clause says nothing of that kind remained on these places.
segments.s107-g49: که is the relative conjunction 'that/which' linking this clause to the previous line. در + بوستانش places the scene 'in its garden' (در = in; بوستان = garden; ش = enclitic possessive 'its'). همیشه is an adverb 'always'. گلست is the poetic copula + predicate (گل + -ست), literally 'is flower/flowery' — here an adjectival/predicative form meaning 'is in flower / is flowery'.
segments.s107-g50: به کوه اندرون literally 'to/into the mountain interior' — a locative expression meaning 'within the mountain' (به + کوه + اندرون). لاله و سنبلست repeats the predicative -ست: لاله 'tulip' and سنبلست 'is full of/suggests hyacinths' — the clause asserts that tulips and hyacinths are present there. Word order is poetic (locative phrase before the nominal predicate).
segments.s107-g51: Two coordinated nominal phrases with omitted copulas: هوا خوشگوار = 'the air (is) pleasant' (hāva + adjective), و links the clauses, زمین پرنگار = 'the earth/ground (is) richly-ornamented' (پرنگار is a compound adjective 'full of ornament'). The copula 'is' is implied (typical in Persian descriptive clauses).
segments.s107-g52: نه ... و نه ... is the standard neither...nor negation pattern: 'neither hot nor cold'. The final همیشه‌بهار is a compound adjective 'always-spring' (literally 'ever-springing/ever-spring-like'), describing the perpetual springlike climate. The clause as a whole states climate moderation and perpetual spring.
segments.s107-g53: نوازنده بلبل is an agentive phrase: نوازنده (agentive -نده) + بلبل 'nightingale' — poetically 'the nightingale that plays' or 'the nightingale plays'. به باغ اندرون is a locative phrase 'in/into the inner garden'. The copula or verb 'sings/plays' is expressed by the agentive form rather than an explicit tense-marked verb, a common poetic device.
segments.s107-g54: This is a parallel agentive phrase to the first clause. گرازنده is an agentive formed with -نده and here functions poetically as 'one that bounds/leaps' or 'one that runs' describing the animal; آهو is the gazelle. به راغ اندرون is the locative 'in the inner râgh/meadow'. Note: the supplied sense lists for گرازنده and راغ do not include the common poetic meanings used here (a bounding/leaping agent and a locality 'meadow'), so the precise senses are treated as poetic usage derived from verbal root meaning 'to bound/charge' and an archaic topographical noun. The clause thus means 'the gazelle bounds in the inner meadow.'
segments.s107-g55: همیشه 'always' modifies the verb-like form بیاساید. بیاساید is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'is free from repose / does not rest' (here rendered 'never sleeps / is never given to slumber'); از marks the source/what it is free from, followed by دو nouns خفت و خوی ('sleep/slumber' and 'disposition/habit/sloth'). The phrase describes perpetual wakefulness/activity or freedom from lethargy.
segments.s107-g56: همه‌ساله 'year-round' sets temporal scope; هرجای 'everywhere/wherever' is a locative generalizer. رنگست is the predicative form (رنگ + -ست) meaning 'is colored/has color' (i.e., colorful), و links to بوی 'scent' — the clause says that in every place, through the year, there is color and fragrance. Again the copula is the poetic -ست.
segments.s107-g57: گلابست is a predicative adjective with the archaic copular sense 'is rose‑scented / like rosewater' (the -ست element functions as a copula). گویی is a parenthetical particle 'as if / one would say' (here treated as an introductory modal), and به جویش روان is a prepositional phrase 'into/along its stream, flowing' where جوی + ش (ش = enclitic possessive 'its') gives 'its stream' and روان is an adjective 'flowing' following the noun.
segments.s107-g58: همی is the frequentative/continuous poetic prefix meaning 'ever/still' attached to the verbal phrase. شاد گردد is a passive/impersonal present form 'is made glad / becomes joyful' (گردد = become/come to be). ز introduces the cause ('by/because of'), بویش = بو + یش (its fragrance), and روان here names the soul/spirit that is affected ('its spirit').
segments.s107-g59: A simple enumerative noun phrase of four month‑names joined by و. The items are listed in sequence (classical poetic catalogue style) and function as a temporal frame for what follows.
segments.s107-g60: همیشه is a temporal adverb 'always'; پر از لاله is an adjective phrase 'full of tulips' where از is the preposition linking 'full' to its contents; بینی is the second‑person present form 'you see' placed before the subject (inverted poetic word order), and زمین is the noun 'the earth' (the thing you see).
segments.s107-g61: همه‌ساله is a temporal adjective/adverb 'yearly/each year' modifying the following noun phrase; خندان‌لب is a compound attributive adjective 'smiling‑lipped' placed before the head noun جویبار 'stream'. The whole is a nominal phrase 'the yearly, smiling stream.'
segments.s107-g62: به هر جای is a prepositional phrase 'to/at every place'; باز شکاری is a compound noun 'بازِ شکاری' = 'a hunting hawk/falcon' (باز = falcon, شکاری = hunting), and به کار is an idiom 'at work / in operation'. The phrase as a whole means 'the hunting hawk is at work in every place' with the components ordered in the concise, elliptic style of poetry.
segments.s107-g63: سراسر means 'throughout' or 'all over' and precedes the emphatic modifier همه; together they quantify the head noun کشور 'country/realm'. آراسته is a predicative adjective 'adorned' (the copula is omitted in poetic style), so the clause reads 'the whole country is adorned.'
segments.s107-g64: ز is the poetic ablative/agentive preposition 'by/from' introducing instruments or means. دیبا و دینار lists those means ('brocade and dinars'), and وز is an archaic contraction (و + ز) meaning 'and from' linking to خواسته ('desired' or 'that which is desired'); the whole phrase gives the materials/by‑means with which the country is adorned. This is a common poetic pattern listing instruments after a predicate adjective.
segments.s107-g1: درخت (noun) is the subject; برومند is a descriptive adjective placed after the noun in classical style ('a noble tree'). چون شد بلند is a temporal clause: چون + شد (3rd sg. of شدن “become”) + predicate adjective بلند, literally ‘when it became tall.’ Word order (noun + epithet, then temporal clause) is typical of older Persian verse.
segments.s107-g2: This is a conditional clause followed by an imperative/admonition. گر آید ز گردون is the protasis: گر (“if”) + آید (3rd sg. present/subjunctive of آمد “come/happen”) + ز گردون (“from the heavens/fate”). برو بر گزند is an exhortation: برو (imperative/old-form present 'go' or 'advance') + بر used with the sense 'against' + گزند 'harm/danger' — literally 'go against the harm/attack the danger.'
segments.s107-g3: شود (3rd sg. of شدن) functions as a copular/ergative-like verb meaning ‘becomes’ and scopes over the following predicates. برگ پژمرده is a noun + postpositive adjective ('leaf, withered'), and و connects the coordinated phrase بیخ سست ('root [becomes] weak'). The verb appears before the predicates in this poetic inversion.
segments.s107-g4: سرش is سر + enclitic ش (possessive 'its'), so 'its head/top'; سوی پستی is a prepositional phrase 'toward lowliness/low place'; گراید (3rd sg.) means 'turns/ inclines'; نخست is an adverb 'first/initially'. The clause therefore says that the tree's top is the first part to lean toward the low/mean direction. Note the possessive enclitic attached to the noun.
segments.s107-g5: چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning 'when'; از جایگه marks the origin 'from its place/position' (جایگه = place/position); بگسلد is the 3rd sg. present/subjunctive of گسستن 'to split/sever' and takes پای خویش ('its foot/base') as the thing that is torn away. The enclitic ش on خوی (rendered here as separate ش) marks possession ('its').
segments.s107-g6: به شاخ نو marks the means/instrument ('by/with a new branch'): به + شاخ (branch) + نو (new). آیین دهد is a verbal phrase 'gives/formulates an order or establishes a custom' (آیین here = 'order, custom; the way of being') with دهد (3rd sg. 'gives/causes') as the main verb. جای خویش = 'its place/position' (جای = place; خوی + ش = 'its own'). The whole means it reestablishes its station through a new branch.
segments.s107-g7: مراو را is an older/poetic pronominal form marked by the object marker را; here it functions as the indirect or benefactive object ('to/for him/it' — the exact referent is set by broader context). سپارد (3rd sg. of سپارَدن) means 'entrusts, leaves, or bestows.' گل و برگ و باغ are the coordinated direct objects 'flower(s), leaf/foliage, and garden.' The particle را makes the preceding noun phrase definite/specific.
segments.s107-g8: بهاری is an adjective/noun meaning 'spring-like' or 'of spring.' به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner of / as' (به marking the relation, کردار = 'deed/manner'). روشن چراغ is a descriptive nominal phrase 'a bright lamp' (روشن = bright, چراغ = lamp). Together the phrase qualifies how the gift appears or what it makes the recipient: spring-like and manifest/bright as a lamp in conduct or appearance.
segments.s107-g9: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). شاخ is the subject ('a shoot/branch') with the adjective بد placed after it (classical word order). خیزد (from خاست) is a present/subjunctive-type form meaning 'arises/springs up'; از بیخ نیک is a prep. phrase 'from the good root' with بیخ 'root' as the source and نیک 'good' qualifying it.
segments.s107-g10: تو is the (poetic) subject 'you'. با introduces the instrument 'with'; شاخ here is used metaphorically as 'horn' or projecting weapon. تندی is an adverbial or noun meaning 'fierceness/violence' modifying the action. میاغاز (classical spelling of می‌آغاز) = 'begin/strike/set to' (imperative/indicative sense with تو as subject), and ریک is an archaic/rare particle left untranslated (an emphatic/uncertain adverbial).
segments.s107-g11: پدر 'father' is the topic. چون here has a temporal/causal sense 'when/as'. به فرزند ماند (به + indirect object + ماند 'be left/remain') expresses that the world/realm becomes the son's (i.e. the domain remains to the son). جهان is the thing that remains ('the world, realm').
segments.s107-g12: کند is the verb 'makes/does'. آشکارا is an adverb 'openly/evidently'. برو بر is a classical idiomatic pairing here meaning 'set/hold the open (over) the hidden' — i.e. prefer or put the manifest above the concealed — and نهان is the opposed noun 'hidden/secret'. The clause thus counsels openness rather than secrecy.
segments.s107-g13: گر is a conditional 'if'. او 'he' is the subject. بفگند (classical/poetic form of فگندن) literally 'casts/throws' and here means 'casts off/destroys'. فر is the specific, traditional word for 'royal glory/splendour', and نام پدر 'your father's name/reputation' completes what is cast off.
segments.s107-g14: This line contains a paired imperative/negative-imperative. تو بیگانه خوانش = 'you call him (ش enclitic)
segments.s107-g15: کرا is an archaic relative/indefinite pronoun meaning 'whoever'. گم شود is a classical verbal phrase: گم (lost) + شود (becomes/is) — here 'becomes lost' (classical present/optative sense). راه آموزگار is a genitival phrase 'the way of the teacher' (راه = way/path; آموزگار = teacher). The word order is inverted for poetry (verb before its genitive complement): literally 'whoever becomes lost the teacher's way' = 'whoever loses/strays from a teacher's path.'
segments.s107-g16: سزد‌گر is the fused classical construction سزد + گر ('it is fitting' / 'if it befit'), used idiomatically to mean 'deserving' or 'it is right that'. جفا بیند = 'meets/encounters cruelty' with جفا (cruelty, injustice) as the object and بیند (classical present of دیدن) meaning 'meets/receives' here. از روزگار marks the source/agent 'from Fate' (از = from; روزگار = fate/times). Together: 'it is fitting that he should meet cruelty from Fate.'
segments.s107-g41: بگفتا که introduces reported speech: بگفتا is a past‑tense narrative verb ‘he said’ (old/poetic past form). که is the complementizer 'that' linking the report to the following noun phrase. رامشگری is a nominal infinitive/abstract noun 'soothing, making calm', and درست follows as an adjective 'proper' describing that means; the small preposition بر here links درست to رامشگری in an adverbial/qualifying sense ('in a proper way' / 'proper').
segments.s107-g42: This clause states the instruments/means of calming. ابا is problematic in the modern lexicon (here likely an archaic or clipped form meaning 'with' or 'by'), so no provided sense matches exactly. بربط names the instrument (barbat/lute), و is the coordinating 'and', نغز is an adjective 'pleasant/delicate', and رامشگرست is a predicative present/factual clause (poetic copula) meaning 'is soothing' or 'is a soother'. Word order places the instruments before the copula.
segments.s107-g43: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded / he said (politely)'. تا here introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that / that', not a temporal 'until'. پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'in his presence / before him'. خواندند is a past‑tense plural verb 'they sang/performed' and completes the purpose clause.
segments.s107-g44: This short clause describes what was done next. بر is the simple preposition 'on/by' (here 'by' or 'at'), رود is used as a place 'river/stream' (giving location), سازانش is a possessive plural noun 'his musicians/players' (سازان + -ش), and بنشاندند is a past plural verb 'they seated' (causative/active past). The order is preposition + location + object + verb (typical Persian)
segments.s107-g45: This temporal subordinate clause opens with به بربط 'on/with the barbat' (به + instrument). چو is an old/poetic conjunction 'when'. بایست here has the sense 'stood / took position / began' (poetic usage rather than modal 'must'), followed by بر ساخت where بر is a preposition 'on/at' and ساخت (past) 'made/caused (to produce sound)'. رود in this context is the string instrument ('rud') rather than a river. The sequence shows an action (taking up the barbat) leading to producing sound.
segments.s107-g46: برآورد is a past verb here meaning 'he brought forth / performed / produced' (accomplished). مازندرانی functions as an agent noun 'the Mazandarani (man/from Mazandaran)'. سرود is a noun 'song/ode' (so the phrase means 'the Mazandarani performed a song'). Word order is verb after subject in this poetic line, but the subject (مازندرانی) follows the verb for metrical/poetic reasons.
segments.s107-g47: که here begins a clause expressing a wish or content of the song. مازندران شهر ما is a nominative phrase 'Mazandaran, our city' (region name + appositive). یاد is a noun 'remembrance/memory', and باد is the optative particle 'may' (classical Persian subjunctive/optative), so the whole segment is an optative formula 'may Mazandaran, our city, be remembered.'
segments.s107-g48: This is a parallel optative clause: همیشه 'always' (adverb) precedes بر و بومش 'its town and soil / its territory' (بر و بوم = town and land), آباد is the adjective 'prosperous/inhabited', and باد again is the optative 'may'. The phrase literally reads 'may its town and land always be prosperous.' Word order places the time adverb first, then the phrase to be wished upon, followed by the adjective and the optative particle.
segments.s107-g193: به آواز is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'aloud' or 'with a voice' that modifies گفتند. گفتند is the 3rd-person plural past tense 'they said/uttered' and introduces reported speech. ما با توایم is the reported clause: ما is the subject 'we', با is the preposition 'with', and توایم is an archaic/fused predicative meaning 'we are with you / we are yours' (a literary substitute for modern با تو هستیم).
segments.s107-g194: ز تو is a prepositional/poetic phrase 'from you' (z = from). بگذرد is a 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive-like form of گذشتن 'to pass' used here in a literary/optative sense 'may/does pass' or more loosely 'passes/let pass'. پند is the noun 'advice/counsel' and appears after the verb in classical word order. کس نشنویم is negative present/subjunctive/optative 1st‑person‑plural: کس 'anyone' and نشنویم 'we do not hear / we do not listen', meaning 'we will listen to no one / we heed no one's advice.'
segments.s107-g195: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject. یکسره is an adverb meaning 'altogether/at once' or 'straightaway' that intensifies همه. نزد شاه آمدند is a verb phrase: نزد in this sense means 'in the presence of / before', شاه 'the king', and آمدند is the 3rd‑person plural past 'they came/approached' — together: 'they all at once came before the king.'
segments.s107-g196: بر is a preposition here meaning 'upon/to/onto' and governs the following noun phrase. نامور is an adjective 'famed/renowned' modifying تخت گاه, a compound 'throne-place' (تخت 'throne' + گاه 'place/seat' used in a poetic/compound sense). آمدند is again the 3rd‑person plural past 'they came' — 'they came upon/approached the renowned throne.'
segments.s107-g17: چنین است X is a common poetic inversion meaning “Such is X” (چنین + copula است). رسم سرای کهن is a nominal phrase: رسم “custom” governs سرای کهن (an attributive phrase where کهن is a postpositive adjective modifying سرای). The genitive/possessive relation between رسم and سرای is expressed by juxtaposition, typical of older poetic Persian.
segments.s107-g18: سرش = سر + enclitic possessive ش “its/his”; هیچ before the verb is emphatic (“not at all”). پیدا is adjectival/participial meaning “visible/apparent,” and نبینی is the classical negative present/subjunctive 2nd-person form (here used generically: “you will not see”/“one does not see”). ز بن = از بن “from the root/base,” where ز is the poetic form of از and بن = “root/foundation.”
segments.s107-g19: چو here is the subordinating conjunction “when/if.” رسم بدش is a noun phrase where بد (“bad”) qualifies رسم and ش is the possessive “its/his.” بازداند is a classical 3rd-person singular verb meaning “restrains/turns back/forbids” (perfective/present sense in literary style). کسی stands as an indefinite subject that, together with the negated verb in the next segment, yields “no one.”
segments.s107-g20: نخواهد is the negative of خواه (here functioning as an impersonal/future-like third-person: “will not/would not wish”); که is the complementizer “that/to” linking to the subordinate clause; ماند is the present/infinitive sense “remain/last”; به گیتی = “in the world”; بسی is an adverbial modifier meaning “much/long” (i.e. “for long”). Together: “no one will want that he remain long in the world” → “no one will want to remain long in the world.”
segments.s107-g21: چو = “when.” کاووس is the subject (proper name). بگرفت is a past/preterite 3rd-person form of گرفتن meaning “seized/took” (here ‘took the throne/office’). گاه پدر is a temporal/genitive phrase “the time/place of the father” (i.e. upon his father’s leaving/death or in his father’s stead). The order is typical older Persian: conjunction – subject – verb – temporal complement.
segments.s107-g22: مر is an archaic dative/benefactive particle meaning “for/to (someone)” (used here before او را to mark that someone’s advantage/possession); او را marks the person (him) as the one who benefits; جهان بنده شد = “the world became a servant/slave” (بنده شد = became servant); سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning “entirely/from head to foot.” The whole phrase reads as “the world entirely became his servant.”
segments.s107-g23: همان ... و هم ... و هم ... is a repeated enumerative structure: همان “the same/that very,” then a list of three items joined by و هم (and also), a common poetic device for emphasis. تخت, طوق, گوشوار are nouns in simple apposition (throne, collar/necklace, earring/ornament). No copula is needed in this nominal listing.
segments.s107-g24: Another nominal phrase: همان تاج زرین = “that same golden crown” (زرین is a postpositive adjective ‘golden’), and زبرجد نگار is a descriptive compound meaning ‘adorned with/embellished by zabarjad (peridot)’—in poetry the linking ezāfe can be omitted and adjectives/nouns stack. نگار here functions as a poetic epithet indicating ornamentation.
segments.s107-g73: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). کاهلی پیشه گیرد is a classical Persian clause where پیشه گیرد ('takes up as a profession/practice') is a present/future-oriented verb phrase with an implicit third-person subject ('one/he'). دلیر stands as the predicate adjective that will be linked to the negated verb in the next segment (the verb that says whether he becomes brave appears after this clause).
segments.s107-g74: نگردد is the negative present/optative form of گشتن ('become') — here functioning as the main verb ('will not become'). ز آسایش و کام سیر is a prepositional/nominal phrase ('satiated by/from comfort and desire') that supplies the second predication; سیر is used adjectivally ('satiated'), and ز is the poetic variant of از ('from'/'by'). The verb نگردد thus applies back to دلیر (he will not become brave) and implicitly also yields 'nor be sated by...'.
segments.s107-g75: من begins the clause ('I'). از marks the comparison ('than' in classical Persian) and introduces the list of rulers (جم و ضحاک و از کیقباد) being compared to the speaker; the subject is explicit here (من) and the compared items are given with از.
segments.s107-g76: فزونم is a first-person predicate meaning 'I am greater / I surpass' (classical present). The repeated به before each noun (بخت, فر, داد) marks the domains in which the speaker exceeds the named kings ('in fortune, in farr/royal glory, and in justice'). Note the classical abstract nouns (فر = farr) used as qualities.
segments.s107-g77: فزون here functions as 'more'/'superior' and is combined with the first-person modal بایدم (an older orthography of بایدَم, 'I must/ought') to state obligation or necessity about the speaker's superiority. زان ایشان is a classical comparative phrase meaning 'than them' (زان functions like 'than / from that'), and هنر is the noun being compared ('excellence' / 'virtue'). Word order is characteristic of older Persian where modality and comparative particles cluster before the noun.
segments.s107-g78: جهانجوی is the subject 'the world-seeker' / 'the ambitious person'. باید is an impersonal/modal 'must/ought' applying to that subject. سر تاجور is a nominal predicate meaning 'the head/chief of merchants' (سر = chief/head; تاجور = merchant), so the whole clause says what an ambitious person ought to be. The structure places the predicate after the modal, typical in classical diction.
segments.s107-g79: چون is used temporally here ('when') — a classical usage; سخن is the subject ('the speech/words'), and به گوش رسید is an idiom meaning 'reached the ears' or 'was heard by'. رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached'. بزرگان is the indirect object ('the great ones' / elders).
segments.s107-g80: از ایشان کس ... ندید is a classical negative existential construction: از ایشان ('of/among them'), کس ('anyone' but rendered 'no one' in combination with the negative verb), and ندید ('did not see'). این رای فرخ is the direct object ('this fortunate counsel/plan'), where رای means 'plan/counsel' and فرخ qualifies it as 'fortunate'. Note that Persian commonly places the object before the verb as here.
segments.s107-g137: همی is the epic habitual/continuous particle placed before the verb, giving a present/future or iterative sense. بگزایدش is the verbal form (ب- prefix + گزاید, 'selects/chooses') plus the enclitic -ش (= 'it'/'him'), so the clause reads roughly 'he/it will choose it'. گنج بی‌رنج is the object phrase 'treasure without toil' (گنج = treasure; بی‌رنج = without hardship/effort) and precedes the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s107-g138: چراگاه (pasture/feeding place) is the noun head, followed by مازندران which specifies whose pasture it is (genitive apposition typical in poetry). بایدش is باید ('must/is fitting') with the enclitic -ش referring back to the treasure; here باید expresses appropriateness/necessity: 'Mazandaran should be its pasture.'
segments.s107-g139: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. هیچ here functions in the litotes/augmentative sense 'even the slightest' or 'any' (an attenuating/insubstantial sense). سرخاری (literally 'a little redness') is used figuratively as 'a small sign/trace', and از آمدن is the prepositional phrase 'of/from the coming/arrival' where از marks the source/relatum.
segments.s107-g140: سپهبد is the noun subject/title. همی again marks habitual/near-future. زود is an adverb 'soon/quickly' placed before the auxiliary. خواهد شدن is a periphrastic future: خواهد (future auxiliary) + شدن ('to become' / 'to be made'), so 'he will soon become / be made (spahbed)'.
segments.s107-g141: همی gives an emphatic/iterative sense. رنج تو is the noun phrase 'your toil/suffering' with تو as the possessive/agentive. داد here is the verbal stem meaning 'give' used in the idiom به باد دادن ('to give to the wind' = 'to squander/waste'); خواهد is the future auxiliary, and به باد is the idiomatic prepositional complement ('to the wind' / 'to naught'). Grammatically this is a verbal periphrasis meaning 'will be wasted.'
segments.s107-g142: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('that/for which' or 'because'). بردی is the simple past, second-person singular of بردن ('you took/won/carried'). ز (poetic form of از) is the preposition 'from', followed by آغاز ('the beginning'). باکیقباد is a fused form meaning 'with/by Kayqubad' (با + کیقباد) functioning as the agent/associate in the phrase.
segments.s107-g143: تو is the explicit subject 'you'. با + رستم is the instrumental/associative 'with Rostam.' شیر ناخورده is a descriptive noun phrase 'an unweaned / not-yet-fed lion' (شیر = lion; ناخورده = not-fed / inexperienced) modifying the subject's manner. سیر here is a nominal sense ('journey' / 'on the march') describing activity in which they engaged — poetic word order places these modifiers before the main verb.
segments.s107-g144: میان را is the definite object 'the middle / the middle-way' with را marking it as definite/direct object. ببستی is the past second-person singular of بستن ('you closed/blocked'). چو is the simile particle 'as/like', followed by شیر دلیر 'a brave/lion-hearted lion' (شیر = lion, دلیر = brave), completing the simile 'like a brave lion.'
segments.s107-g65: بتان پرستنده is a literary noun phrase: بتان (the idols) together with the active participle پرستنده functions as ‘idol‑worshippers’ (the noun preceding the participle). با introduces an accompanying attribute (‘with’); تاج زر is a noun + material ('crown of gold') where زر qualifies تاج.
segments.s107-g66: همه نامداران is ‘all the famed/renowned ones’ (همه = all). به here marks the state or dress ('in/with') and links to the following nominal phrase زرین کمر (‘golden girdle/belt’), so the phrase means ‘all the famous (men) in/with golden girdles.’
segments.s107-g67: چو is a temporal conjunction ‘when’. کاووس is the subject (Kāvus). بشنید is the past 3rd‑person form ‘he heard’. از او marks the source/agent ‘from him’, and این سخن is the object ‘this speech’ — canonical past narrative order in classical Persian.
segments.s107-g68: یکی تازه اندیشه is an indefinite noun phrase ‘a certain/fresh thought’. افگند is the past 3rd‑person verb ‘he cast/caused’ and takes بن (‘foundation/root’) as its complement — an idiomatic classical expression meaning he ‘gave rise to’ or ‘laid down’ a new idea/plan.
segments.s107-g69: دل رزمجویش is a genitive/attributive phrase: دل (‘heart’) modified by رزمجویش (‘his warlike one’ / ‘his warrior’). ببست is the past 3rd‑person verb ‘he bound/tied’ (used figuratively = he resolved/firmed his heart). اندر‌ان = ‘among them / therein’ indicates the sphere or group where this resolve was set.
segments.s107-g70: که introduces a subordinate or purpose clause (‘that/so that’). لشکر is the direct object or theme ‘army’. کشد here is the classical present/subjunctive form of کشیدن meaning ‘to draw/lead/march’ (the provided lexeme list does not include this sense, so it is marked unknown). سوی = ‘toward’, and مازندران names the destination/target.
segments.s107-g71: چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so’. گفت is the past verb ‘he said’ (the sense list given treats گفت as a noun for ‘conversation’, so that specific verbal sense is not in the provided list and is marked unknown below). با introduces the addressees ‘with/among’ — سرفرازان رزم = ‘the proud/victorious ones of battle’ (compound: adjective + battle noun).
segments.s107-g72: که introduces reported content (‘that’). ما is the subject ‘we’. سر نهادیم is past 1st‑person plural of نهادن (‘we set/laid [our] heads’) — an idiom meaning ‘we devoted/committed ourselves’. یکسر is an adverb ‘entirely/altogether’. به marks direction/aim ‘to’, and بزم here means an assembly/session (hence ‘committed ourselves to the undertaking/assembly’ in context).
segments.s107-g97: که is the relative/connecting 'that/who' introducing a clause about Jamshid. جمشید is the proper name (subject). با is the preposition 'with' indicating accompaniment or instrument. فر is the technical Old/Iranian term 'farr' (royal glory) and انگشتری is a concrete noun 'a ring' — the phrase says Jamshid possessed or acted 'with farr and a ring'. Word order is subject then instrument phrase.
segments.s107-g98: به فرمان او is an ablative/agentive phrase meaning 'by his command' (به used in the sense 'by/‑ly'); فرمان 'order/command' and او 'he'. The list دیو و مرغ و پری are direct objects or governed beings: 'demons, birds, and fairies' linked by و 'and'. The main verb is omitted (typical of epic Persian) and understood as 'had/ruled/was master of'.
segments.s107-g99: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' marking the domain (ز مازندران = 'of/from Mazandaran'). یاد ... نکرد is the verbal phrase 'did not mention/remember' (یاد کردن = to mention/remember); هرگز is the negator 'never', and نکرد is the past negative 'did not do'. Word order places the domain before یاد and the negation follows as the verb.
segments.s107-g100: نجست is the past of جستن used here in the sense 'sought'/'looked for' and takes از دلیران 'from among the brave' (از = 'from', دلیران = 'the brave' or 'warriors'). دیوان نبرد is a genitival phrase 'battle with (the) devs' where دیوان names the opponents and نبرد is 'battle'. The clause thus says he did not seek (or summon) from the brave a battle against the devs. Note: the printed sense list for دیوان in the data does not include 'demons', so that token's semantic mapping below uses -1.
segments.s107-g101: This is a nominal phrase naming Fereydun and attributing two adjectives: پردانش 'learned' and پرفسون (an uncertain/obscure epithet in the text). و simply joins the two adjectives. The phrase describes Fereydun's qualities before the next clause.
segments.s107-g102: همین را uses را as the definite/object marker with همین 'this/that (very thing)'. روانش 'his soul/spirit' is the subject (with the possessive -ش). نبد is an archaic/poetic negative copula 'was not / did not exist as', and رهنمون is the predicate 'a guide'. Literally: 'This was not to his spirit a guide.'
segments.s107-g103: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. شایدی is a modal adverb here meaning 'it is possible' or 'you may be able'. بردن is the infinitival/root verb 'to bear/ to endure' (in the idiom بَسر بردن = to endure/spend life), and این بد بسر is the object phrase 'this ill/evil to the life' — together meaning 'to endure this misfortune'. Word order places the conditional and modality before the infinitive phrase.
segments.s107-g104: به here is the preposition 'to/with/through'. مردی can mean 'man' but in epic usage often denotes 'manliness/valor' (here taken as the quality/reward). گنج 'treasure' and then another به introduces a parallel set 'to/for renown and skill' (نام = reputation/fame, هنر = skill, excellence). The segment lists means or ends (qualities, rewards) related to the preceding conditional.
segments.s107-g153: که ماند is a rhetorical interrogative: که (relative/interrogative 'who/that') + ماند (3rd-person of ماندن 'remain/stand'). The following که introduces the subordinate clause giving the thing that would happen; از + تیغ او marks the agent/instrument ('by his sword'), and در جهان is a locative adverbial 'in the world'. Word order is poetic (verb before subject of the following clause) and the second که is a complementizer linking to the verb بلرزند.
segments.s107-g154: بلرزند is 3rd-person plural (subjunctive/jussive/general present) 'they tremble/would tremble'; یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether' modifying the verb. کهان و مهان is a fixed poetic pair meaning 'the lofty and the low / all ranks' (here 'the world’s great and small'); the verb agrees with that plural/those collective notions.
segments.s107-g155: نباشد شگفت is an impersonal negative clause meaning 'it would not be a wonder/it would not be surprising' (نباشد = 'would not be'); ار is the conditional 'if'; بمن is the prepositional object 'to me'; نگرود (classical form of گرویدن/گرو) = 'he turns to/accepts' (here subjunctive/conditional 'he should turn/accept'). The clause expresses a hypothetical, so the verb forms are optative/subjunctive.
segments.s107-g156: شوم is 1st-person optative/subjunctive 'may I become' or 'I would be'; خسته‌گر is an archaic/poetic conditional/adjectival phrase (خسته + گر) functioning like 'if [one] becomes weary' or as an intensifier in this conditional sense; پند من نشنود = 'he does not hear my counsel' (نشنود = 3rd-person negative of شنیدن 'to hear/listen to'). Together the line means 'may I be undone if he does not heed my advice' — a classical optative/conditional formula.
segments.s107-g157: ورین is a demonstrative/adverbial 'this aforementioned' or 'on this (foregoing)'; رنج is the noun 'suffering/sorrow'; آسان کنم is 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I make easy' (کنم = 'I do/make'); بر دلم is the locative/benefactive phrase 'on my heart' meaning 'in my breast' or 'to my feelings'. The clause means 'if I ease this sorrow upon my heart.'
segments.s107-g158: از اندیشه شاه is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from the thought/consideration of the king' (از = 'from, away from'); دل بگسلم is 1st-person present/subjunctive/future of گسستن with the causative/preterite stem 'بگسلم' = 'I will break/part/sever my heart' — i.e. 'I will detach my heart from thinking of the king.' The verb b- prefix is the classical present/future marker in such verbs.
segments.s107-g159: نه ... پسندد is a negation: نه introduces negation here. از من پسندد literally 'likes/approves of me' (پسندد = 3rd-person present of پسندیدن 'to like/approve'); جهان‌آفرین 'the world-creator' (God) is the subject placed after the verb for poetic emphasis/word order inversion. The clause therefore states that the Creator does not approve of me.
segments.s107-g160: نه ... و نه is the correlative conjunction 'neither ... nor'; شاه and گردان ایران‌زمین are the coordinated nouns 'the king' and 'the troops/hosts of the land of Iran' — the verb from the prior clause is understood to apply to them as well (i.e. they too do not approve). This is a compressed, poetic coordination.
segments.s107-g121: مگر introduces a conditional exception meaning 'unless'. زالش is زال with the possessive/connecting -ش and names the person (Zal). آرد is an archaic/poetic 3rd‑person singular present/past‑narrative form meaning 'brings' (here used as the protasis verb). ازین is از + این (written as از + ین in poetry) meaning 'concerning/of this'. گفته is a noun 'word/statement' and here the object of آرد. باز follows the verb as an adverb meaning 'back/again' (i.e. 'bring back word').
segments.s107-g122: وگرنه is a simple connective meaning 'otherwise'. سرآمد here functions as a noun 'climax' or 'summit/conclusion'. نشان is 'sign/mark' and takes سرآمد as its qualifier ('the summit of the sign'). فراز is an adverbial/postpositive element meaning 'on high' or 'above', completing the noun phrase.
segments.s107-g123: سخنها is the plural object 'words/speeches' (note the plural -ها). ز is the poetic variant of از 'from/of' and combines with هر گونه ('every kind') to mean 'of every sort'. برساختند is past tense, 3rd‑person plural, meaning 'they made/composed/produced' (a compound verb formed with the prefix بر‑).
segments.s107-g124: هیونی is a poetic plural/collective meaning 'beasts/monsters' or 'savage creatures'. تکاور is an agent noun 'raider/elite warrior' and here stands alongside هیونی as another subject. برون is an adverb 'out/forth'. تاختند is past tense 3rd‑person plural 'they rushed/charged/attacked', so the whole clause means they rushed out.
segments.s107-g125: رونده is a present‑participle/agent noun 'the one who is moving' (here 'rider' or 'runner'). همی is the older continuous/iterative marker indicating ongoing action ('kept on / was still'). تاخت is the past tense 'galloped/rushed'. تا + نیمروز is a temporal expression 'until noon' (تا = 'until', نیمروز = 'midday').
segments.s107-g126: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. آمد is past 'came'. بر with a person is the classical locative/prepositional use 'upon/on' (i.e. 'to/at'). زال is the proper name. گیتی فروز is an epithet attached postpositively to زال: گیتی 'world' + فروز 'illuminating/glorious', i.e. 'the world‑illuminating (one)'.
segments.s107-g127: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. داد here is the past 3rd‑person verb 'gave' (used with پیام: 'gave/sent a message'). از introduces the source 'from'. نامداران is a plural agent 'the renowned/illustrious ones'. پیام is the direct object 'message/tidings'. The word order (adverb + verb + source + object) is typical of epic style.
segments.s107-g128: که‌ای here introduces the quoted address (classical/poetic vocative), roughly 'that: O...'. نامور is the vocative adjective 'renowned/famed'. با + گهر is an adjectival phrase 'with jewel(s)' i.e. 'adorned with jewels'. پور is the patronymic element 'son (of)' attached to the name سام: پور سام = 'son of Sam' (a common name‑forming element in epic Persian).
segments.s107-g25: This is a noun phrase: 'همان' (demonstrative/emphatic 'that/those') precedes the modified noun 'تازی اسپان' ('Arabian horses'); the adjectival phrase 'آگنده یال' follows and describes them ('with manes full/abundant mane'). Word order is typical Persian: determiner + noun + modifiers; 'آگنده' is a descriptive adjective modifying 'یال'.
segments.s107-g26: 'به گیتی' is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world'; 'ندانست' is the past form with negative prefix (classical poetic past of دانستن) meaning 'knew not' or 'did not find'; 'کس را' (with object marker را) functions as the (here negatively intended) object 'anyone/no one'; 'همال' is a noun meaning 'match/peer' (used figuratively: 'anyone equal to them'). The clause reads literally 'in the world knew no one a match'.
segments.s107-g27: 'چنان ... که' is the correlative construction 'so... that'; here 'چنان' = 'such/so'. The word written 'بد' is a poetic/archaic form standing for the past of بودن (بُد = 'was') — not the common adjective 'bad' — so the phrase means 'it was such that'. 'در گلشن زرنگار' is a locative phrase 'in the brocaded/gilded garden' where 'زرنگار' post-modifies 'گلشن'.
segments.s107-g28: 'همی' is an old/prosodic particle that gives iterative or continuous sense (here a habitual/iterative past nuance); 'خورد' is used in the sense 'befell/ happened to' (classical use of خوردن); 'روزی' functions as the thing that befalls ('a day' / 'a fortunate day') and 'می‌خوشگوار' is an adjectival description 'pleasant/delightful' modifying that 'روزی'. Together: 'a delightful day still befell (there)'.
segments.s107-g29: 'یکی' here is an indefinite 'one / a certain'; it introduces 'تخت زرین' ('golden throne'). 'بلورینش' is 'بلورین' (crystalline) plus the possessive suffix '-ش' = 'its/his', so this noun phrase describes a particular throne as 'golden and crystalline (of his)'; 'پای' (literally 'foot/base') here anticipates location ('at the foot/of which') and links syntactically to the following verb phrase.
segments.s107-g30: 'نشسته' is the past participle/predicate 'seated/sat'; Persian often puts participles in predicative position in poetry. 'بروبر' is a prepositional/adverbial compound meaning 'over/upon' or 'on the brow of'; 'جهان' is its object 'the world'. 'کدخدای' is in apposition to the subject (the occupant): 'the kadhkhoda (headman) sat over the world' — i.e. he sat upon that throne as lord/overseer of the world.
segments.s107-g31: In this poetic line 'ابا' functions as a variant of 'با' meaning 'with' (an archaic/metrical form). 'پهلوانان ایران' is the noun phrase 'the champions of Iran'. 'به هم' ('to one another' / 'together') reinforces the sense of collective action. The whole segment means 'with the Iranian champions together'.
segments.s107-g32: 'همی' is the iterative/prosodic particle giving a habitual sense ('would, used to'); 'رای زد' is a light-verb collocation where 'رای' (counsel/decision) combines with زد (literally 'to strike') to mean 'gave a decision/deliberated' (a common classical usage). 'شاه' is the subject 'the king'; 'بر' introduces the topic 'concerning'; 'بیش و کم' literally 'more and less' is an idiom meaning 'matters great and small' (i.e., all sorts of issues).
segments.s107-g105: منوچهر is the proper name 'Manuchehr' (subject/accusative depending on verb); کردی is the past tense 2nd-person singular 'you made/you placed'. بدین here functions as 'in/with this' (classical prepositional phrase; the tokenizer split it into ب + دین). پیشدست is a noun/adverb meaning 'vanguard/forefront' or 'in advance' and completes the idea that someone was put into the leading post.
segments.s107-g106: نکردی is the negative past 'you did not (make/do)'. The following cluster is essentially بر این بر دل خویش پست = 'upon this, upon your own low heart' — بر این (here tokenized as بر + ین) = 'upon this'; the second بر is the preposition 'on/upon' introducing the phrase بر دل خویش 'on your heart'; خویش + ش is the possessive (own / his) and پست is an adjective 'low/base' modifying دل. Together the clause says you did not make him base in your own heart.
segments.s107-g107: یکی چاره = 'one remedy' or 'a single solution' (numeric determiner + noun). باید is the modal 'must / it is necessary', and کنون 'now' positions the sentence in time. اندرین is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in this / among these' specifying the domain: 'now there is one remedy in this (matter)'.
segments.s107-g108: که introduces a content clause 'that ...'. این بد = 'this evil' (این 'this' + بد 'evil/bad'). بگردد is a classical subjunctive/optative form meaning 'be turned/removed/drive away' (here 'be driven away' or 'be turned aside'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and ایران‌زمین is 'the land/realm of Iran'.
segments.s107-g109: چنین 'thus/so' opens the speech. گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said/spoke' (the provided sense list had noun senses; grammatically this is the verb 'he said'). پس 'then' is temporal/organizational. طوس is the personal name Tūs (here the hero, not the city). با is the preposition 'with', and مهتران is the plural 'chiefs/leaders' (here 'the elders/chiefs' whom Tūs addresses).
segments.s107-g110: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory particle here ('O' or 'you who are...') introducing direct address. رزم دیده is a compound adjective 'battle-seen' = 'battle-tested/experienced in war' (رزم 'battle' + دیده past participle 'seen' used adjectivally). دلاور is 'brave/heroic' (here functioning as an epithet), and سران is the plural noun 'leaders' (vocative plural: 'O brave, battle-tested leaders').
segments.s107-g111: مراین بند is an older/classical phrasing meaning 'this bond/knot/problem' (مر + این بند; the exact nuance of مراین is archaic, roughly 'of/for this'); را is the object marker marking بند as the topic/object. چاره اکنون یکیست = 'the remedy now is one' (چاره 'remedy', اکنون 'now', یکیست 'is one'). The construction places the solution as a single, present remedy for the matter.
segments.s107-g112: بسازیم is a hortatory/subjunctive 1st-person-plural 'let us make/do/arrange' (a proposal/suggestion). و 'and' links the clauses. این کار 'this work/affair' is the subject of دشوار نیست 'is not difficult' — نیست is the negative copula. The speaker therefore proposes action and immediately downplays its difficulty.
segments.s107-g33: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رامشگری is a noun identifying the kind of being (here 'one who soothes/entertains') and stands in apposition to دیو. زی is the archaic preposition 'of/from' linking دیو to پرده‌دار, so پرده‌دار ('curtain-keeper' or chamberlain) further characterizes the demon.
segments.s107-g34: بیامد is the past tense 'came'. که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who/that ...'. خواهد is the classical present/future-form of خواستن meaning 'wishes/ intends'. بر is the preposition 'to/upon', شاه 'king' is its object, and بار is a noun used poetically ('burden/occasion' — here roughly 'to appear/present oneself before') that completes the idea 'wishes to present/come before the king.'
segments.s107-g35: چنین گفت is the fixed phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' in context). کز is the archaic fused form of 'که + از' here meaning 'from' or 'who is from', introducing the origin phrase 'from the city of Mazandaran.'
segments.s107-g36: یکی is an existential/indefinite 'one' / 'a certain one'. خوشنوازم is the predicative adjective/noun 'a skilled or pleasant musician' in an older/poetic form (the -م ending here belongs to the poetic clause pattern). ز marks the partitive 'of/from', so ز رامشگران means 'one of the ramashg̱arān' (the soothe-makers/comforters).
segments.s107-g37: اگر is the conditional 'if'. در خورم is an idiomatic/classical phrase meaning 'it befits me' or 'is proper for me' (خورم is an archaic/intransitive verbal form 'to befit' here). بندگی شاه را is the definite object 'the serving/servitude of the king' with را marking it as definite.
segments.s107-g38: گشاید is a classical/poetic form meaning 'may/will open' (from گشودن) in the third person; بر تخت او specifies the destination 'upon/to his throne', and راه را is the definite object 'the way' with را. Word order here (verb + prepositional phrase + object) is a poetic variation but all elements link as 'he will open the way to his throne.'
segments.s107-g39: برفت is an archaic/past verbal form 'he departed/left' (the supplied senses list does not reflect this verbal sense). از بر پرده is a stacked prepositional phrase 'from beside/off the curtain' (از + بر + noun). سالار بار is an epithet/title in apposition ('chief/commander of the baggage/retinue'); grammatically these nouns are appositive descriptors attached to the departing person.
segments.s107-g40: خرامان is an adverb/adjectival adverb meaning 'with a proud/stately gait'. بیامد is the past 'came'. بر شهریار is the prepositional phrase 'before/to the sovereign/king' indicating the person he approached.
segments.s107-g161: شوم here is an optative/jussive-like particle built on شدن used before the verb phrase to mark the speaker's intention or wish (‘let me / I will’). گویمش is گویم (1st‑person present/subjunctive of گفتن) plus the enclitic ـش = 'him/her', so the clause literally reads 'let me tell him'. هرچ آید ز پند is a dependent nominal clause: هرچ 'whatever' + آید (present/subjunctive 'comes/happens') + ز پند 'from advice', giving 'whatever comes from advice.'
segments.s107-g162: ز من گر is a conditional circumstantial phrase 'if (it is) from me' (ز = 'from', گر = conditional 'if' attached here to indicate 'if [he takes it] from me'). پذیرد is subjunctive/potential 'accepts' and بود functions as a copular/conditional auxiliary ('would be'), with سودمند as the predicate adjective 'useful' (so: 'if he accepts it from me, it would be useful').
segments.s107-g163: وگر is the conditional 'and if'. تیز گردَد is an impersonal/third‑person present/subjunctive construction 'becomes sharp/keen/swift' (تیز as predicative adjective, گردد = 'becomes'). گشادست راه is an inverted verbless clause: گشادست 'is wide' + راه 'the road/way', so 'the way is wide.' The inversion (adjective before noun) is common in poetry.
segments.s107-g164: تهمتن is a proper epithet/name (Tahmatan/'mighty one') and هم = 'also'. ایدر (ایدر) = 'here', followed by بود 'was' (simple past copula) and با سپاه 'with an army' (با = with, سپاه = army). The clause states that Tahmatan was also present here with troops.
segments.s107-g165: پر اندیشه is a fixed adjectival phrase 'full of thought' (پر = 'full (of)', اندیشه = 'thought'). بود is the past copula 'was'. آن شب دیرباز places the time: آن شب 'that night' and دیرباز 'long/long‑lasting', so 'that long night was full of thought.'
segments.s107-g166: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun' and بنمود is the simple past 'showed/revealed'. تاج از فراز is the object phrase 'the crown from on high' (از فراز = 'from above/on high'), so the clause means 'when the sun displayed the crown on high.'
segments.s107-g167: کمر بست is an idiom 'he girded his loins' (کمر = waist, بست = bound/tied). و links the two verbs. بنهاد سر is the past 'he placed/put (his) head' (بنهاد used here as 'put/laid' and سر = head), i.e. 'bowed his head'. سوی شاه is the directional phrase 'toward the king' (سوی = toward, شاه = king).
segments.s107-g168: بزرگان is the subject 'the great ones/nobles'. برفتند is the past plural 'they went/set out'. با او = 'with him' and به راه = 'on the road' or 'set out on the way' (به راه رفتن = to depart), so the nobles accompanied him on the road.
segments.s107-g177: This clause consists of a short verb phrase plus the object and verb-of-exclamation: برو is here an imperative/elliptical motion-word (literally “go”/“away”) used in narration to mark movement or attention; سرکشان is the plural noun ‘the proud/rebellious ones’ acting as the object/target of praise; آفرین is an interjection (‘Bravo!’ / an acclamation) and خواندند is the past-tense 3rd-person plural verb ‘they cried/sang/called’. Word order is object/interjection before the past verb (typical of poetry), so literally “(to) the proud ones they cried ‘Bravo!’.”
segments.s107-g178: سوی شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning “toward the king” (سوی = toward, شاه = king). با او is a prepositional phrase ‘with him’; همی is the archaic/progressive particle that often appears before a past verb to mark continuous or simple past action; راندند is the 3rd-person plural past of راندن ‘to drive/ride’ (here ‘they rode’). Together: “they rode with him toward the king.”
segments.s107-g179: بدو is a short prepositional dative ‘to him/to that one’ (classical form). گفت is the past verb ‘said’ (here used as the reporting verb). طوس‌ای is the vocative form ‘O Tus’ (Tū­s, the hero/place-name); گو is the imperative/present stem ‘say/speak’; سرفراز is a vocative epithet ‘O proud/victorious one’. The clause reads as reported speech: ‘Tus said to him: “O noble one, speak.”’
segments.s107-g180: کشیدی is the 2nd-person past tense ‘you endured/suffered’; چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so’; رنج is the noun ‘hardship/suffering’; راه دراز is a noun phrase ‘the long road’ (راه = road/path, دراز = long). Grammatically this is a simple accusative construction: ‘you endured such a hardship — (of) the long road.’
segments.s107-g181: ز بهر is a poetical prepositional phrase meaning ‘for the sake of’ or ‘on behalf of’ (ز = from/on account of, بهر = for); بزرگان is the plural ‘the great ones / nobles’; ایران‌زمین is the compound noun ‘the land of Iran’ (the Iranian realm). The whole phrase marks the beneficiary/cause: ‘for the nobles of Iran.’
segments.s107-g182: برآرامش is a compound prepositional/nominal phrase ‘for (the) peace/calm (of)’ (بر + آرامش). این رنج is the direct object ‘this hardship’. کردی is the 2nd-person past ‘you did/made/caused’; گزین here is the verbal root ‘to choose’ used as the lexical complement — together (کردی گزین) conveying ‘you chose/you have chosen.’ Word order is poetic/inverted but yields: ‘you chose this hardship for the sake of their peace.’
segments.s107-g183: همه سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplicative phrase meaning ‘all, one and all / entirely’; نیک‌خواه is the noun ‘well-wisher’ or adjective ‘well-disposed’; توایم is an archaic 1st-person present/predicative form meaning ‘I am yours / I am with you’ (here expressing devotion on behalf of the speaker/group). The clause reads: ‘We (or I, on behalf of all) are entirely your well-wishers.’
segments.s107-g184: ستوده is a past participle/adjective ‘praised, honoured’; به is the preposition ‘by/through/with’; فر is the important poetic term ‘farr’ meaning royal/divine glory or radiance; کلاه is literally ‘helmet’ or ‘cap’ (here ‘helmet’); توایم repeats the archaic predicative ‘I am yours’. Grammatically this is an ablative/agentive phrasing: ‘praised by the splendour (farr) of your helmet, I am.’
segments.s107-g129: یکی کار پیش آمد اکنون شگفت is a compact clause: 'یکی کار' = 'a certain event/affair', and 'پیش آمد' is a verbal collocation meaning 'occurred' (پیش + آمد). اکنون is a temporal adverb 'now', and شگفت is an adjective ('strange, marvelous') describing the event. Word order is topic (one/one-event) + verb phrase + temporal/adjective comment — typical of epic Persian.
segments.s107-g130: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('that'). آسانش = آسان + enclitic -ش ('its/easy it') meaning 'its being easy' or 'easy it', here best read as 'easy' with the pronoun. اندازه is a noun 'measure/extent', نتوان is the negative present/past auxiliary 'cannot' (classical negative of توانستن) and گرفت is the past infinitive/verb 'to take/seize' used idiomatically with اندازه = 'to take/assign a measure' → 'cannot be measured'.
segments.s107-g131: برین is the clitic combination of بر + این ('on/for this'), so 'for this'. کار‌گر here functions as the affair/task (poetic usage) or as a nominal referring to the work; تو is the 2nd-person subject. نبندی is present/subjunctive negative 'do not bind' (from بستن) and کمر refers to 'waist / girdle' — the idiom کمر بستن = 'to gird oneself, prepare (for effort)'.
segments.s107-g132: نه ... نه is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor'. تن means 'body/person' and ماند is the verb 'remains/is left' (past/stative sense here 'will remain' in narrative). ایدر = 'here'. بوم و بر is a fixed pair meaning 'home and land' (homeland/abode); و is the conjunction 'and', and بر functions as the second member of the idiom. The clause warns that neither body nor home will remain here.
segments.s107-g133: یکی = 'a certain', شاه را uses the definite object/ topical marker را attached to 'the king' (here marking the theme), and بر دل اندیشه خاست literally 'a thought rose upon/into the heart' — خاست is the past 'arose/began'. Word order places the emerging thought as the event affecting the king.
segments.s107-g134: بپیچیدش is a past-form verb with the third-person object enclitic -ش ('him/it'); the verb means 'twist/turn' (here 'twisted him/turned him aside'). آهرمن is the agent (Ahriman, the devil) following the verb — a normal archaic order V + object + subject. از راه راست = 'from the straight way/path' (از = from, راه = way, راست = straight/true).
segments.s107-g135: به introduces a circumstance or cause 'by/through/on account of'; رنج means 'suffering/hardship', and نیاگانش = 'his ancestors' (نیاگان + enclitic -ش 'his'). از باستان 'from ancient times' specifies antiquity. Together they form a causal/time phrase 'because of his ancestors' ancient suffering'.
segments.s107-g136: نخواهد here functions as negating/future auxiliary 'will not' (classical usage of خواستن/خواست as auxiliary), همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('still/indeed' in classical style), and بود is the copula 'be'. همداستان is a predicate adjective 'like-minded / in agreement'. The clause therefore reads 'he will not be like-minded' (i.e., will not agree/be of one mind).
segments.s107-g113: This is a noun phrase that functions as the object of the verb in the next segment. The sequence is a name/epithet (هیونی تکاور) followed by a prepositional phrase (بر زال سام). In classical Persian the preposition بر comes before its objects (زال و سام) and here means 'toward/to' those people.
segments.s107-g114: بباید + verb is an impersonal necessity construction: 'it is necessary (that) ... be sent.' فرستاد is the main verb in an infinitive/unspecified form meaning 'to send/dispatch' and is governed by بباید. The coordinate phrase و دادن پیام uses دادن as an infinitive/nominal verb ('to give' / 'to deliver') linked by و and parallels فرستاد (i.e., 'send and deliver a message').
segments.s107-g115: که‌گر is the contracted classical conditional 'که اگر' = 'if'. The clause سر به گل داری is literal word order: 'head (سر) + به (towards/into) + flower (گل) + داری (you have)' — an idiomatic image meaning being enamoured or given to beauty. اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. مشوی here is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'do not be' / 'do not remain' (negative exhortation); it is predicative and addresses the second person.
segments.s107-g116: This is a sequence of imperatives directed at the addressee. یکی here functions adverbially ('once' / 'for a moment' / 'one (time)'); تیز کن is a causative/imperative combination (تیز = 'sharp', کن = 'make/do') so 'sharpen' (the) مغز ('brain, mind') is the direct object. The following و links a second imperative بنمای ('show! / make appear!') with روی ('face') as its object: 'show your face.'
segments.s107-g117: مگر here means 'unless' (introducing a negative-exceptive condition). کاو is a classical third-person element ('he' / 'that one') in this clause (the provided lexical senses are not aligned; functionally it is a pronoun). گشاید is a present/subjunctive/optative third-person singular of 'to open' (classical form) meaning 'opens/should open.' لب پندمند is a noun + adjective ('an admonishing lip' / 'an admonitory mouth') and is the direct object of گشاید.
segments.s107-g118: سخن ('speech/words') is the subject or theme; بر دل is the prepositional phrase 'upon the heart (of...)' and شهریار بلند is a noun + postpositive adjective ('the lofty/high sovereign')—classical Persian often places adjectives after the nouns they qualify. Together the phrase says the admonitory speech will fall upon or reach the king's exalted heart.
segments.s107-g119: بگوید here is a classical/historical-present reporting verb ('he says' / 'it is said') introducing indirect speech; the provided lexical senses do not match the verbal use, so functionally it is the reporting verb. که is the complementizer 'that'. این اهرمن is the demonstrative + proper name 'this Ahriman (the Devil)'. داد یاد is a verb + noun sequence where داد (gave) is the verb and یاد ('reminder/warning') is its object: 'gave a warning/reminder.'
segments.s107-g120: در دیو is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/within a demon'; هرگز is the adverb 'never'; نباید is the impersonal negative modal 'must not/should not'; and گشاد is a predicate adjective/participle meaning 'opened' (so نباید گشاد = 'it must not be opened'). The sequence expresses a prohibition: 'one must never open (it) in a demon.'
segments.s107-g185: This is a fronted clause: the speaker and addressees are named. زال (Zāl) is the speaker and comes at the end for emphasis; چنین ('thus') modifies قال/گفت. ابا here is archaic/poetic and does not behave like modern با; it introduces the relation to the named people (the noblemen).
segments.s107-g186: This is a relative/clausal continuation introduced by که: هر کس که = 'whoever'. او را is the accusative (him) marked by را. نفرسود is a past participle/perfect (فرسود) with a negative prefix here ('not worn/consumed'), and سال ('year') is placed at the end for poetic word order: literally 'whoever the year has not worn down.'
segments.s107-g187: همه ('all') modifies پند ('advice'). پیرانش is پیران + -ش (his elders), so 'the advice of his elders.' آید به یاد is an idiom meaning 'comes to mind' or 'is remembered' (آید = 'comes'). Word order is Subject (همه پند پیرانش) + predicate (آید به یاد).
segments.s107-g188: ازان پس ('from that time on') is a temporal phrase. دهد is the present/optative form of 'to give' used here in a poetic sequence before the final past داد ('gave'); چرخ گردانش ('the revolving/turning wheel' with the enclitic -ش) is a personified fate/sky, and داد is the simple past 'gave'—together: 'from then on the turning wheel gave (to him).' The doubled verb forms are a poetic usage to emphasize the action of bestowal by fate.
segments.s107-g189: نشاید is an impersonal negative predicate 'it is not fitting/it should not be.' It is followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که, where گیریم (subjunctive/optative first person plural of گرفتن) expresses the (disallowed) action 'that we take.' ازو is the archaic contraction of از او ('from him'), and پند باز places the object (advice) with باز ('again') following it.
segments.s107-g190: کزین = ک + از + این ('from this' / 'by this'). The clause states the reason: پند (this counsel) + ما (we) + نیست (is not) + خود بی‌نیاز (ourselves free of need). In other words, 'for by this counsel we ourselves are not without need' (we are not self-sufficient), so we should not take counsel from him.
segments.s107-g191: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'because of.' پند و خرد‌گر = 'advice and the wise man(s)' (خردگر here denotes a wise person), and بگردد is a third-person singular present/subjunctive of گردیدن 'to turn' (here 'his head will turn' i.e., he will be led astray). سرش is 'his head' with the enclitic -ش; the verb precedes the subject for poetic inversion.
segments.s107-g192: پشیمانی ('regret') is the subject, آید ('comes') is the verb, ز گیتی ('from the world') gives the source or cause, and برش is بر + -ش ('upon him'); together: 'regret comes upon him from the world.' The clause states the consequence of turning away from counsel.
segments.s107-g145: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now' placed at the start to set the scene. آن همه is a demonstrative + quantifier 'all that / that whole (multitude)'. باد شد uses a noun + شد (become) construction: باد (wind) with شد 'became', a poetic metaphor meaning the host dispersed or became like wind. پیش اوی is a prepositional phrase 'before him', where اوی is an archaic third‑person enclitic pronoun ('him') attached after the preposition.
segments.s107-g146: بپیچید is the past (narrative) form of پیچیدن 'to twist/coil' (here 'twisted' or 'reeled'). جان is the direct object 'life, soul'. بداندیش is an adjective/noun 'malicious, ill‑intentioned' modifying جان, and اوی is the enclitic 'his/that of him' marking possession/reference; together: 'the life of the ill‑willed one twisted.'
segments.s107-g147: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'. دستان is the plural noun 'hands' (subject here). بپیچید is the verb 'twisted' agreeing with that plural subject. سخت is an adverbial intensifier here ('strongly/violently') modifying the verb.
segments.s107-g148: تنش is the noun 'his body' formed by تن + enclitic ش 'his'. گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became'. لرزان is a predicative adjective 'trembling'. بسان is a comparative preposition 'like/as', followed by درخت 'tree' to form the simile 'trembling like a tree.'
segments.s107-g149: همی is a frequentative/progressive particle used before past verbs in epic Persian to indicate continuous or habitual action ('kept / was continually'). گفت is the past of گفتن 'said' and introduces the reported statement. کاووس is the proper name 'Kavus'. خودکامه is an adjectival predicate 'self‑willed / autocratic' and مرد is the predicate noun 'man' (the copula 'بود' is omitted, as is common: 'Kavus was a self‑willed man').
segments.s107-g150: نه ... نه is the correlative pair 'neither ... nor'. گرم here means 'fervent / zealous' (not merely 'warm') and آزموده is a past participle used adjectivally 'proven/experienced'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from / by', and گیتی is 'the world'. سرد is the opposite pole 'cold' or 'aloof', so the whole clause qualifies the earlier characterization.
segments.s107-g151: کسی is an indefinite pronoun 'anyone / someone'. کاو here is an archaic/poetic relative particle meaning 'who / that' (not the verb 'to dig'); because the provided lemma senses don't match this function, that entry is marked -1. بود is the past copula 'was'. در جهان is the prepositional phrase 'in the world'. پیش گاه is a compound meaning 'in former times / before' (پیش 'before' + گاه 'time'). Together the clause asks rhetorically if anyone existed before him in the world.
segments.s107-g152: برو is the verb 'go' (here narrational/jussive flavor) and بگذرد is the third‑person form of گذشتن 'to pass' — together they form a poetic sequence meaning 'go and pass' or simply 'pass by'. سال و خورشید و ماه lists the temporal nouns 'year(s), the sun, and the moon' as things that (continue to) pass, emphasizing the passage of time.
segments.s107-g81: همه زرد گشتند و پرچین بروی is a coordinated past action: همه (subject) + زرد گشتند is a past-tense intransitive clause formed with گشتند (past plural of گشتن 'become'). The following phrase و پرچین بروی is a poetic juxtaposition/ellipsis: پرچین (a descriptive noun/adjective here) and بروی (variant/compound meaning 'on the face' or 'appeared') form a secondary predicate describing their faces; the verb 'شد' is omitted as is common in epic poetry.
segments.s107-g82: کسی جنگ دیوان نکرد آرزوی is a single clause where کسی is used in a negative sense ('no one'), جنگ دیوان is the object ('war/battle' + دیوان), and نکرد is the past negative/perfective main verb (did not do). آرزوی sits after as an adverbial/nominal gloss 'as (his) wish' — the phrase means 'no one waged the battle of the divs as (he) wished.'
segments.s107-g83: کسی راست پاسخ نیارست کرد is a past clause with topicalization: کسی ('no one') + راست پاسخ ('a straight/true answer') is the direct object, and کرد is the past auxiliary. نیارست is an archaic/poetic verbal form here linked to کرد (the exact etymology is irregular in epic language), so the whole reads 'no one made/gave a true answer.'
segments.s107-g84: نهانی (adverb) modifies the following nominal clause: روان‌شان is روان + possessive suffix -شان ('their souls/spirits'), and پر از + N means 'full of N' (here پر از باد سرد = 'full of cold wind'). Word order is the typical Persian noun phrase 'روان‌شان پر از باد سرد' meaning 'their spirits were filled with cold wind.'
segments.s107-g85: This is a poetic list introduced by repeated چو (here 'as' / 'like'), enumerating heroes. Each چو marks a simile or comparison and the names follow in coordination with و. The verb is omitted — the line simply names those who are being compared/present.
segments.s107-g86: Another equals-list with چو beginning and a string of names joined by و. The sequence is primarily nominal (names) with no finite verb — a common epic technique to pile up heroes. The final item (نیو) is presented as a name in the list.
segments.s107-g87: به آواز is an adverbial phrase ('in a voice' = aloud). گفتند is the past plural verb 'they said'; ما کهتریم is the quoted content ('we are lesser/inferior'). In Persian reported speech the quoted clause follows the reporting verb without quotation markers in older texts.
segments.s107-g88: زمین جز به فرمان تو نسپریم is a negated volitional/declarative clause: جز به فرمان تو ('except by your command') is the exception phrase, and نسپریم is the first-person-plural present/subjunctive form of سپردن with negative prefix ن- here meaning 'we will not entrust/surrender (the land)'. The subject is implied (we), so the whole means 'we will not hand over the land except at your command.'
segments.s107-g169: خبر شد is a fixed past phrasing meaning “it became known / news spread.” The preposition به marks the recipients (dative/indirect object) — here a list of proper names follows (طوس، گودرز، گیو). Word order is topical: خبر شد + به + recipients.
segments.s107-g170: This continues the same dative list introduced by the initial به: the preposition governs رهام and the following coordinated items. گردان here functions as a noun 'troop' (modifying or linked with the final word), and نیو appears as the final name/element in the phrase (likely a proper name or epithet) forming the last item of the list.
segments.s107-g171: که introduces a subordinate temporal clause here (≈ 'when' / 'as'). دستان is the grammatical subject (a poetic/collective term; here 'Dastān' or 'the forces/party'), followed by the prepositional phrase به نزدیک ایران 'near Iran'. رسید is simple past 'arrived/reached'.
segments.s107-g172: درفش همایونش is a noun phrase: درفش 'standard/banner' modified by همایونش ('his auspicious/royal'). آمد پدید is a poetic two-word predicate meaning 'came forth/appeared' (آمد = came, پدید = into view).
segments.s107-g173: پذیره شدندش is a poetic/periphrastic reception-phrase: پذیره (reception) + شدند (they became/made) + ـش (object clitic 'him') → 'they received him / welcomed him.' سران سپاه ('the chiefs of the army') is the agent/subject placed after the verb phrase for poetic word order.
segments.s107-g174: This clause describes an action on the head: سری 'head' (object), کاو is an archaic/poetic form equivalent to 'که او' or 'that he' (third-person marker), and کشد is the past of کشیدن used transitively here ('drew/put on'). پهلوانی کلاه is a genitive/attributive sequence meaning 'the warrior's/heroic helmet' (adjective-noun order is poetic); overall: 'he put the hero's helmet on his head.'
segments.s107-g175: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' دستان سام is a genitive/compound meaning 'the forces/party of Sam.' اندر آمد is a compound verb 'entered / came in,' and به تنگ means 'into the narrow place / pass' (تنگ = narrow/pass).
segments.s107-g176: پذیره شدندنش is a nominalized/verbal phrase meaning 'their reception / that they were received' (شدندنش = their becoming/were made); همه 'all/everyone' and بی‌درنگ 'without delay / immediately' modifies the clause: 'they were all received immediately.'

sentences.s107-s45: After that they held a single assembly, and they gave their hearts to his words.
sentences.s107-s46: They sat and talked with one another, asking what fate had befallen us.
sentences.s107-s47: If the king will not hide the words he has spoken while drinking, ...
sentences.s107-s48: Destruction arose from us and from Iran; none was left on this land, on the water, or on the soil.
sentences.s107-s25: that its garden is always in bloom; within the mountain grow tulips and hyacinths.
sentences.s107-s26: The air is pleasant and the ground richly adorned; neither hot nor cold, it is ever-spring.
sentences.s107-s27: The nightingale plays in the inner garden; the gazelle bounds in the inner meadow.
sentences.s107-s28: It never sleeps nor is given to sloth; all year round, everywhere there is color and scent.
